quick_facts.label: Role scope
quick_facts.value: A Senior UI/UX Designer leads user-centred interface and experience work for web and mobile products: understanding requirements, researching users, mapping flows, producing wireframes and prototypes, and iterating designs with engineering and product teams.

quick_facts.label: Typical seniority evidence
quick_facts.value: In Indian public-sector digital-product job descriptions, comparable UI/UX roles commonly ask for roughly 3–5+ years of relevant experience; the exact threshold, domain exposure, and title vary by employer and vacancy.

quick_facts.label: Common qualification routes
quick_facts.value: Employers may seek a bachelor’s degree in design, HCI, visual communication, or a related field; some also accept graduates who have a relevant UI/UX diploma or certification. A demonstrable portfolio is explicitly requested in comparable roles.

quick_facts.label: Core deliverables
quick_facts.value: Common deliverables include user research outputs, personas, journeys and flows; information architecture; wireframes, mock-ups and interactive prototypes; and design guidelines, component libraries, or design systems.

quick_facts.label: Accessibility expectation
quick_facts.value: For public-facing Indian digital platforms, UI/UX work may require responsive, multilingual and accessible interfaces aligned to GIGW, WCAG and related accessibility requirements.

sections.heading: What a Senior UI/UX Designer does
paragraphs: The role owns substantial parts of the end-to-end product-design process rather than only producing screens. Comparable Indian digital-government role descriptions include gathering and analysing user needs through interviews, surveys and usability studies; creating personas, journey maps and user flows; developing information architecture; and translating the resulting requirements into wireframes, mock-ups and interactive prototypes for web and mobile products.

Senior-level practice also includes maintaining coherence across a product. This can mean creating or maintaining design guidelines, patterns, component libraries and design systems; testing and refining designs using feedback and usability evidence; and working with product, engineering and QA colleagues so that the released implementation reflects the intended experience. For public digital services, accessibility and responsive behaviour are material design responsibilities, not merely visual polish.
bullets: Plan and conduct, or synthesize, user research and usability findings.
Turn complex journeys and functional requirements into understandable flows, interaction models and prototypes.
Set and apply reusable UI patterns, visual rules and component-library standards.
Review implementation with engineers and iterate against user feedback, product goals and accessibility requirements.
Explain design trade-offs clearly to cross-functional stakeholders.

sections.heading: Entry routes in India
paragraphs: There is no single statutory licence or one mandatory degree for this occupation. A common route is a bachelor’s education in design, visual communication, HCI or a related discipline, followed by project, internship or junior UI/UX work. Comparable government job descriptions also allow a graduate with a relevant UI/UX diploma or certification, showing that demonstrable capability can complement formal design education.

For progression from UI/UX Designer to senior practice, build a portfolio that shows the reasoning behind real or realistic product work: the user problem, research or evidence used, flows and prototypes explored, design-system decisions, usability iterations, accessibility considerations and the delivered outcome. A portfolio is specifically identified as a requirement in a comparable MyGov UI/UX Designer description.
bullets: Pursue design/HCI/visual-communication education, or combine another graduation route with focused UI/UX training.
Build evidence through internships, freelance projects, campus projects, open-source or case-study work.
Start in UI/UX, product-design or related digital-design roles and seek ownership of complete flows rather than isolated screens.
Maintain a portfolio that makes process, collaboration, iteration and outcomes inspectable.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: Senior UI/UX Designers need a combined research, interaction-design and visual-design skill set. Indian role descriptions identify user research, personas, journeys, user flows, storyboards, process flows, sitemaps, wireframing, mock-ups and prototyping as practical capabilities. They also expect a designer to convert complex concepts into intuitive interfaces and communicate the rationale for design decisions.

Tool proficiency is useful but should support sound design practice. Figma, Adobe XD and Sketch are named in comparable descriptions, alongside responsive web design, colour and layout principles, and working knowledge of HTML, CSS and JavaScript. Accessibility knowledge is particularly valuable where products serve diverse public audiences; some government-platform roles expressly call for alignment with GIGW and WCAG.
bullets: User research, synthesis, personas, journey mapping and usability testing.
Information architecture, interaction design, task flows, wireframes and interactive prototyping.
Visual hierarchy, typography, colour, responsive layouts and reusable UI components.
Design-system governance: patterns, libraries, documentation and consistency checks.
Figma or comparable prototyping tools; working literacy in front-end constraints and developer hand-off.
Accessibility-aware design, including keyboard, assistive-technology and responsive considerations.
Stakeholder communication, facilitation, prioritisation and collaboration in Agile or cross-functional teams.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is done
paragraphs: Senior UI/UX Designers work on digital products such as websites, mobile applications and large-scale platforms. The work is inherently cross-functional: comparable Indian job descriptions place the designer alongside product managers, developers, QA and other stakeholders, with responsibilities spanning requirements gathering, design production, feedback-led iteration and implementation coordination.

Sector context can affect the design constraints. Digital-government roles may require multilingual, responsive and standards-aligned interfaces; product roles may also value experience with mobile and web applications, Agile/Scrum working, and domain familiarity such as fintech, edtech, health-tech, e-commerce or government services.
bullets: Product and engineering collaboration is central to the role.
Work may cover web, mobile and responsive interfaces rather than a single channel.
Large public-facing services can add multilingual and accessibility requirements.
Domain knowledge can be advantageous when workflows are complex or regulated.

sections.heading: Progression from UI/UX Designer
paragraphs: The transition from UI/UX Designer to Senior UI/UX Designer is usually evidenced by broader ownership and stronger judgement: independently handling complex user journeys, making research-informed decisions, establishing reusable patterns, reviewing implementation and coordinating design work across disciplines. This is an inference from the scope and experience requirements in comparable Indian UI/UX vacancies, rather than a nationally regulated promotion standard.

Further progression can move toward lead, manager or design-strategy responsibilities, where the emphasis shifts toward coordinating design activity, maintaining a coherent design language and aligning experience design with organisational goals. Titles and ladders vary widely across employers, so candidates should compare the actual scope, reporting line and portfolio expectations in each vacancy.
bullets: UI/UX Designer: develops core research, prototyping and interface-design capability.
Senior UI/UX Designer: owns complex flows and quality across delivery; strengthens systems and cross-functional influence.
Potential later paths: design lead, UX manager, product-design manager or UX strategy roles, depending on employer structure.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation for a Senior UI/UX Designer in India is not standardised and should be evaluated vacancy by vacancy. It can vary with years of relevant product-design experience, portfolio strength, city, employer type, sector, accessibility or domain expertise, and the level of ownership expected. For example, comparable public digital-platform advertisements have described pay as either commensurate with qualifications, skills and experience or as per market standards rather than publishing a fixed role-wide amount.

Use dated salary figures, if supplied by a recruiter or job board, only as local market context—not as a guaranteed benchmark. Ask whether the quoted figure is fixed annual CTC, includes variable pay, and reflects the responsibilities of a senior individual contributor versus a team-management role.
bullets: Compare total CTC structure, fixed versus variable components and benefits.
Match the offer to scope: research ownership, system ownership, stakeholder responsibility and any people-management expectations.
Treat published figures as date-specific and employer-specific.
